map.condition: Classify a critical point of f(x,y) (two-variable)

map.use: \text{Check } f'(x)=0 \text{ interior points and endpoints } f(a),\, f(b)

formulas.name: Definition of a Critical Point
formulas.latex: x_0 \text{ is a critical point if } f'(x_0) = 0 \text{ or } f'(x_0) \text{ does not exist}
formulas.plain: x0 is a critical point if f'(x0) = 0 or f'(x0) is undefined
formulas.whenToUse: First step to finding local/global extrema
formulas.trap: Points where f' is undefined (cusps, corners) are also critical points — don't only solve f'=0.

formulas.name: First Derivative Test
formulas.latex: \begin{cases} f'\text{ changes } + \to - \text{ at } x_0 & \Rightarrow \text{local max} \\ f'\text{ changes } - \to + \text{ at } x_0 & \Rightarrow \text{local min} \\ \text{no sign change} & \Rightarrow \text{neither} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: If f' changes + to - at x0 => local max; - to + => local min; no change => neither
formulas.whenToUse: When second derivative test is inconclusive or f'' is hard to compute
formulas.trap: Must check the sign of f' on BOTH sides of x0, not just at x0.

formulas.name: Stationary Point vs Critical Point
formulas.plain: Stationary points (f'=0) are a subset of critical points
formulas.trap: All stationary points are critical, but not all critical points are stationary.

formulas.name: Second Derivative Test (Single Variable)
formulas.latex: \text{If } f'(x_0)=0:\quad \begin{cases} f''(x_0) > 0 & \Rightarrow \text{local minimum} \\ f''(x_0) < 0 & \Rightarrow \text{local maximum} \\ f''(x_0) = 0 & \Rightarrow \text{inconclusive} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: If f'(x0)=0 and f''(x0)>0 => local min; f''(x0)<0 => local max; f''(x0)=0 => test fails
formulas.whenToUse: Fastest classification of a stationary point when f'' is easy to compute

terms.means: second derivative at the stationary point

formulas.trap: When f''(x0)=0, the test is inconclusive — must use higher-order tests or sign-change method.

formulas.name: Higher-Order Derivative Test
formulas.latex: \text{If } f'(x_0)=\cdots=f^{(n-1)}(x_0)=0,\; f^{(n)}(x_0)\neq 0:\\[4pt] \begin{cases} n \text{ odd} & \Rightarrow \text{inflection (not extremum)} \\ n \text{ even, } f^{(n)}>0 & \Rightarrow \text{local min} \\ n \text{ even, } f^{(n)}<0 & \Rightarrow \text{local max} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: When f'=f''=...=f^(n-1)=0 at x0: if n odd => inflection; if n even and f^(n)>0 => min; f^(n)<0 => max
formulas.whenToUse: When the second derivative test (and possibly more) are zero at the critical point

terms.means: order of the first non-zero derivative at x0

formulas.trap: f(x)=x^4 at x=0 has f''(0)=0, but it IS a minimum (n=4, even, positive).

formulas.name: Inflection Point Condition
formulas.latex: f''(x_0) = 0 \text{ and } f'' \text{ changes sign at } x_0 \Rightarrow x_0 \text{ is an inflection point}
formulas.plain: f''(x0)=0 AND f'' changes sign at x0 => inflection point (concavity changes)
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying where concavity switches from up to down or vice versa
formulas.trap: f''(x0)=0 alone is NOT enough; f'' must also change sign. E.g., f=x^4 has f''(0)=0 but no inflection.

formulas.name: Closed Interval Method
formulas.latex: \text{Global extrema of } f \text{ on } [a,b] = \max/\min\{f(a),\, f(b),\, f(x_i) : f'(x_i)=0\}
formulas.plain: Evaluate f at endpoints a, b and all interior critical points; pick the largest/smallest
formulas.whenToUse: Finding absolute max/min on a closed bounded interval

terms.means: interior critical points where f'=0 or f' DNE

formulas.trap: Forgetting to evaluate at the ENDPOINTS — the global extremum may occur there, not at an interior critical point.

formulas.name: Extreme Value Theorem
formulas.latex: f \text{ continuous on } [a,b] \Rightarrow f \text{ attains its global max and min on } [a,b]
formulas.plain: A continuous function on a closed bounded interval always has a global max and min
formulas.whenToUse: Guaranteeing existence of absolute extrema before searching for them
formulas.trap: Does NOT apply on open intervals — e.g., f(x)=1/x on (0,1) has no max.

formulas.name: Critical Points (Two Variables)
formulas.plain: Set both partial derivatives fx and fy to zero simultaneously
formulas.whenToUse: Finding candidates for local extrema of f(x,y)

terms.means: partial derivative of f with respect to x
partial derivative of f with respect to y

formulas.trap: You need BOTH partials to be zero — just one being zero is not sufficient.

formulas.name: Second Derivative Test — Discriminant D
formulas.whenToUse: Classifying a critical point (x0,y0) of a two-variable function
formulas.trap: D must be evaluated at the critical point, not at a general (x,y).

formulas.name: Second Derivative Test Classification (Two Variables)
formulas.latex: \begin{cases} D > 0,\; f_{xx} > 0 & \Rightarrow \text{local min} \\ D > 0,\; f_{xx} < 0 & \Rightarrow \text{local max} \\ D < 0 & \Rightarrow \text{saddle point} \\ D = 0 & \Rightarrow \text{inconclusive} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: D>0, fxx>0 => min; D>0, fxx<0 => max; D<0 => saddle; D=0 => test fails
formulas.whenToUse: After computing D at a critical point of f(x,y)

terms.means: second partial at the critical point

formulas.trap: When D<0 it is a saddle point — NOT an extremum. Don't confuse with the inconclusive case D=0.

formulas.name: Saddle Point Definition
formulas.latex: D < 0 \Rightarrow (x_0,y_0) \text{ is a saddle point: local max in one direction, local min in another}
formulas.plain: D<0 at a critical point means it is a saddle point (neither max nor min)
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying saddle points after computing the Hessian discriminant
formulas.trap: A saddle point is a critical point but NOT a local extremum.

formulas.name: Lagrange Multiplier Condition
formulas.plain: grad f = lambda * grad g, with constraint g(x,y)=0
formulas.whenToUse: Optimizing f(x,y,...) subject to an equality constraint g(x,y,...)=0
formulas.trap: Must also satisfy the constraint g=0 — the Lagrange equations alone are not sufficient.

formulas.name: Lagrange System of Equations (2-variable)
formulas.plain: Solve: fx = lambda*gx, fy = lambda*gy, g(x,y)=0 simultaneously
formulas.whenToUse: Setting up Lagrange multiplier equations for two-variable constrained optimization
formulas.trap: There are 3 equations and 3 unknowns (x, y, lambda) — don't forget lambda is also unknown.

formulas.name: AM-GM Inequality (optimization shortcut)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding min of sum or max of product without calculus, or verifying calculus results
formulas.trap: Equality (and hence the extremum) holds only when a = b, not in general.

formulas.name: Concavity and Second Derivative
formulas.latex: f''(x) > 0 \Rightarrow f \text{ concave up (cup)}; \quad f''(x) < 0 \Rightarrow f \text{ concave down (cap)}
formulas.plain: f''>0 => concave up (bowl shape, local min tendency); f''<0 => concave down (arch, local max tendency)
formulas.whenToUse: Interpreting the geometry of the function around a point
formulas.trap: Concave up does NOT guarantee a minimum — only does so at a stationary point.

formulas.name: Common Power Function Critical Behaviour
formulas.latex: f(x)=x^n:\quad \begin{cases} n \text{ even} \geq 2 & \Rightarrow \text{global min at } x=0 \\ n \text{ odd} & \Rightarrow \text{inflection at } x=0 \end{cases}
formulas.plain: x^n with n even: global min at x=0; n odd: inflection at x=0 (no extremum)
formulas.whenToUse: Quick recognition of behavior of polynomial terms at origin
formulas.trap: x^3, x^5, etc. have f'=0 at origin but it is NOT an extremum — it is an inflection point.

formulas.name: Monotone Function Has No Interior Extremum
formulas.latex: f \text{ strictly increasing (or decreasing) on } (a,b) \Rightarrow \text{no local extremum in } (a,b)
formulas.plain: A strictly monotone function has no local max or min in the open interval
formulas.whenToUse: Ruling out extrema on an interval where f' does not change sign
formulas.trap: The global extremum on [a,b] must then lie at the endpoints a or b.
